title: From Steering Vectors to Conceptors: Compositional Affine Activation Steering for LLMs

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a principled activation-steering framework for large language models based on conceptor theory. The authors derive closed-form optimal linear and affine steering functions that act as soft projections (and optionally translations) onto concept-specific activation subspaces, and show how Boolean algebra over conceptors (AND/OR/NOT) can be used to construct compositional steering objectives. Empirically, conceptor-based steering consistently outperforms standard additive steering on in-context “function vector” tasks, shows advantages on composed functions via Boolean combination, and yields promising results on a complex alignment-relevant behavior benchmark; an additional residual application (I + C) is proposed and argued to better match transformer inductive biases.
strengths: Introduces a theoretically grounded derivation of optimal linear and affine steering operators from first principles, explicitly connecting activation steering to conceptor theory with closed-form solutions.

Extends steering beyond single-direction additive updates to full linear operators that encode rich covariance structure; proposes residual conceptor application (I + C) to better align with additive-residual architectures.
Leverages the Boolean algebra of conceptors (AND/OR/NOT) for principled multi-objective composition, going beyond heuristic vector arithmetic used in prior work.
Evaluates across two families of models (transformers and an SSM) and multiple tasks: standard function-vector tasks, newly constructed composite functions, and an alignment-relevant behavioral task.
Includes ablations comparing linear vs affine conceptors and a head-to-head against a training-based LoRA baseline at similar intervention sites.
The causal/steering setup and the contrast with additive methods are well-motivated and clearly explained with helpful geometric intuition.
The role of the aperture parameter and the nature of conceptors as soft projections are articulated clearly.
Steering and control of LLMs at inference-time are central and active problems; a closed-form, data-efficient, compositional method with strong empirical results is practically valuable.
The Boolean composition operators are a compelling and principled tool for multi-attribute/control scenarios where vector arithmetic is limited.
weaknesses: The “optimality” objective for linear/affine conceptors minimizes distortion on concept-positive activations but does not include an explicit term that moves concept-negative activations toward the target; the steering effect on negatives is thus indirect and not part of the optimization criterion.

Notational inconsistencies and minor mathematical issues: e.g., mixing second moment vs covariance in derivations/implementations; a typo in the NOT operator line (¬C = I − C but printed as “-C = I − C”); unclear conditions under which Σ−1 is used (though later reduced to I − C).
Potential numerical/stability issues for large D: full D×D conceptor matrices may be ill-conditioned with small sample sizes; shrinkage/regularization beyond aperture are not thoroughly discussed.

Missing comparisons to contemporary, stronger learned or nonlinear steering baselines (e.g., Steering Vector Fields, INNSTEER, FLAS, UNISTEER) that also address multi-attribute control and context dependence; LoRA is an odd comparator for inference-time steering and details are sparse (e.g., “full rank LoRA” phrasing).

Limited statistical reporting (error bars, significance) in main figures; some results rely on single best-layer selections and coarse hyperparameter search, notably in open-ended generation where conceptors underperform.

The composite-function dataset is synthetic (GPT-4o generated) with little characterization (noise, difficulty, linguistic diversity). Reported gains on the most difficult composition (English–French & antonyms) appear numerically small; practical significance is unclear.
Some imprecision about where in the stack residual conceptors are applied vs standard conceptors in different experiments, and whether (I + C) or C was used for each result.
More specifics would help on computational overhead (per-layer costs, memory) and sample complexity vs additive steering.

Recent steering frameworks that learn context-dependent, multi-layer, or flow-based operators (SVF, INNSTEER, FLAS, UNISTEER) are not compared against; discussion is limited relative to this growing literature.
Attention-level steering approaches aimed at mitigating KV-cache contamination (e.g., GCAD) are not considered; this matters for long-form or multi-turn stability.
reviews.questions:
1. Your “optimal” objective preserves concept-positive activations but does not explicitly pull negatives toward the concept. Can you clarify the intended steering mechanism for negatives and provide ablations (e.g., with held-out negatives) showing that residual application or Boolean combinations supply this attraction in practice?
2. Which estimator did you ultimately use in all experiments: centered covariance or uncentered second moment? Please provide an ablation (and a theoretical note) on how centering affects C and downstream performance.
3. Did you apply C or (I + C) in each experiment? Can you include an ablation quantifying the benefit of residual application and how it interacts with layernorm and stability in longer generations?
4. How robust are your results to the aperture α and sample size n? Do you use additional shrinkage (e.g., Ledoit–Wolf) or low-rank truncation to stabilize Σ estimates for high-dimensional layers with limited data?
5. For the composite functions, could you compare against stronger vector-composition baselines (e.g., the function-vector arithmetic from Todd et al., or sequential application) and report dataset quality metrics (noise rate, lexical diversity)?
6. Can you elaborate the LoRA baseline setup (rank, optimizer, training steps, data size)? Why is LoRA the right comparator for an inference-time method? Could you instead compare to recent learned steering baselines (SVF, INNSTEER, FLAS, UNISTEER)?
7. On the behavioral benchmark, can you add variance estimates and a broader layer/strength sweep for open-ended generation? Also, did you examine KV-cache effects in multi-turn settings (cf. GCAD)?
8. How costly is conceptor application at inference relative to vector addition (per token per layer)? Are there practical speed/memory strategies (e.g., low-rank SVD) that preserve most gains?
9. For the NOT/AND/OR operators, how do you choose α when composing conceptors trained with possibly different apertures and sample sizes? Any sensitivity analysis?
10. Can you share eigenvalue spectra or principal directions of learned conceptors to illustrate interpretability and the degree of subspace selectivity across tasks?
reviews.assessment: This paper offers a compelling and principled extension of activation steering from additive vectors to conceptor-based linear and affine operators, with a clear theoretical foundation, interpretable composition via Boolean algebra, and strong improvements on standard function-vector tasks. The residual formulation is a thoughtful architectural match, and the initial results on a complex behavioral task are promising. However, two issues temper the strength of the contribution at NeurIPS standards: (i) the “optimality” criterion focuses on preserving positives, leaving the mechanism for moving negatives toward the concept largely implicit rather than explicit in the learning objective; and (ii) the experimental evaluation omits comparisons to several recent, stronger learned/nonlinear steering baselines that specifically target multi-attribute control and context dependence. Clarity could be improved by standardizing the statistical reporting and disambiguating centered vs uncentered estimators, as well as detailing baseline setups (especially LoRA) and computational costs. Overall, I find the work novel enough and practically useful—particularly the compositional Boolean calculus and the closed-form, data-efficient operators—to be of interest to the community. With stronger baselines, clearer statistics, and explicit ablations addressing the concerns above, this paper would make a solid contribution.
